focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

Sticker glue rolled into gray boogers under Lena's thumbnail as she scraped dead price stickers off a shelf. She had spent the first hour of her shift restacking cheap prosecco; rain had kept at it all night and was turning the cardboard cases in Farah's front window soft at the corners. The shop smelled the way it always did when the weather went wet: cork dust waking up, orange peel from the register dish, a cold mineral note where somebody had slopped sauvignon on the concrete and only mostly mopped it. It was already past three. She liked the scraping better than checking her phone. She kept checking it anyway.

The last thing Eli had sent was a photo from somewhere he said was a load-in call in Atlantic City. Black T-shirt, half grin, one hand on the edge of a road case with a strip of fluorescent tape across it. Behind him, a cinder-block wall the color of old dishwater. It looked normal enough. That was the problem. The spaces around him had started looking normal in the exact same way, each one generic and just detailed enough to excuse not being looked at twice. Last night on video he had pushed his hair back, laughed at something she said, and then a breath later the call had given her the push and the laugh again, exact to the knuckle against his temple. She had blamed lag because the alternative was uglier and because everybody blamed lag for everything now.

She zoomed in on the photo until the pixels went soft around his jaw.

Farah came out from the back carrying a roll of shelf tags and a box cutter. She never let a crooked handwritten sign survive in her line of sight; she straightened one with the flat of her thumb before she even looked at Lena. Rain had silvered the curls at her temples darker than usual. She smelled faintly of clementi...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ved on instinct and the numbers in her head became a lower, steadier ache.

By then she no longer needed the legal pad to do the math. She was twelve hundred short for October 15, the pawn ticket gave her ninety days, and twenty-five dollars was gone just to push the money through. His name kept showing up with those numbers now, sliding in beside rent and the Visa minimum whether she wanted it there or not.

That did not thin the love. It only dirtied it, which was worse.

Around eight, Signal lit the desk.

Eli: You fed me and saved me in the same day. That's a lot of woman.

It was exactly the kind of line that would have melted her yesterday. Tonight it did something more complicated and more final. It warmed her. It disgusted her. It made her put the phone face down and hold the bare finger against her mouth until the cucumber-melon smell had finally gone.

At nine she left the laptop open on the desk, washed the mug, and put the legal pad in the drawer with her passport, the Wildwood paperwork, and the envelopes she used for taxes. She left the pawn ticket under the lamp. She wanted to see it when she woke up. She did not trust any version of herself that would have tried to tidy this away overnight.

Near midnight, another message came through: a picture of coiled cable on a concrete floor, the toe of his boot in one corner.

Eli: load-out's a mess. i'll call when i can.

In bed, she set the phone on the pillow beside her face and left one earbud in. The bare finger kept brushing the sheet as if looking for its usual resistance. The absence had texture. It snagged her attention every time she turned over. She stayed awake longer than she meant to, stupidly alert for the phone, until the lit lamp and the stalled caption file on the desk finally blurred into sleep.
